The Art of Unix Programming
Chapter 1: Philosophy — Philosophy Matters

Culture? What Culture?

This is a book about Unix programming, but in it we're going to toss around the words
'culture', 'art', and 'philosophy' a lot. If you are not a programmer, or you are a
programmer who has had little contact with the Unix world, this may seem strange. But
Unix has a culture; it has a distinctive art of programming; and it carries with it a
powerful design philosophy. Understanding these traditions will help you build better
software, even if you're developing for a non-Unix platform.

Every branch of engineering and design has technical cultures. In most kinds of
engineering, the unwritten traditions of the field are passed on only by personal
apprenticeship to skilled practitioners, and through the example of good work. But
Unix has unusually strong, distinctive, and yet informal cultural traditions. Because
so much of Unix is open source, you can read the codes and reuse the tools in ways
that strengthen and spread the culture.

The Durability of Unix

Unix was born in 1969 and has been in continuous production use ever since. That's
long past the end of the life cycle of most proprietary operating systems. Unix
survived its original owner (AT&T) being broken up, being bought out, being sued
repeatedly, having its source code leaked, and having its trademarks fought over in
court — all while being increasingly copied and cloned by open-source competitors.
Today Unix is still the premier operating system for serious computation, scientific
research, the Internet backbone, large-scale servers, and a multitude of embedded
systems. Learning to program in the Unix way is likely to be useful for the rest of
your career.

The Unix Philosophy: A Brief Survey

The Unix philosophy is not a formal design method. It wasn't handed down from the
high fastnesses of theoretical computer science as a way to produce theoretically
correct software. Nor was it invented by some master programmer who then required all
others to follow it. No, the Unix philosophy is bottom-up, not top-down. It is
pragmatic and grounded in experience. It is not a set of abstract principles that
programmers are supposed to piously follow; rather, it consists of engineering
habits, preferences, and accumulated folk knowledge about what works and what doesn't
in software development.

Doug McIlroy's summary of the Unix philosophy is perhaps the most famous:

  Write programs that do one thing and do it well.
  Write programs to work together.
  Write programs to handle text streams, because that is a universal interface.

These rules were written by Doug McIlroy in 1978 and still capture the spirit of
Unix software design. They suggest a culture of tools, of small composable programs,
of pipelines, of simplicity and modularity.

Rule of Modularity: Write simple parts connected by clean interfaces.

The most important rule, the one that underlies all the others, is the Rule of
Modularity: Write simple parts connected by clean interfaces. This rule recommends
building complex systems from small, simple modules. It recommends using clean
interfaces between these modules so they can be understood independently. And it
recommends writing simple code, not just simple modules, because simplicity is a
virtue in itself.

Rule of Clarity: Clarity is better than cleverness.

Because maintenance is so important and so expensive, write programs as if the most
important communication they do is not to the computer that executes them but to the
human beings who will read and maintain the source code in the future (including
yourself). Code that cannot be easily understood by future developers is not
maintainable. Code that is not maintainable is a burden, not an asset.

The Rule of Composition: Design programs to be connected to other programs.

It's hard to avoid programming overcomplicated monoliths if none of your programs can
talk to each other. Unix encourages developers to write programs that can be
connected together in pipelines and networks. This suggests using ASCII text as a
universal interface format. Data in textual form is easy to inspect, filter, and
transform. Tools that speak the same textual language can be combined in countless
ways.

Rule of Separation: Separate policy from mechanism; separate interfaces from engines.

In designing interfaces, it is usually good practice to separate the interface from
the engine. An interface presents a simplified, abstracted view of the underlying
engine. It hides the complexities of the engine from the user. It provides a stable
external contract even when the internals change. This is the principle of
information hiding, and it is central to good software design.

Rule of Simplicity: Design for simplicity; add complexity only where you must.

Many pressures encourage complexity in software. Marketing wants more features. Users
want more capability. Engineers want to exercise their skills. Managers want to show
progress. All of these forces push toward bigger, more complex systems. But
complexity is the enemy of reliability, maintainability, and security. Simpler
systems are easier to understand, easier to test, and easier to fix when something
goes wrong.

Rule of Parsimony: Write a big program only when nothing else will do.

Rule of Transparency: Design for visibility to make inspection and debugging easier.

A software system is transparent when you can look at it and immediately understand
what it is doing and why. Transparency is the first step toward debuggability.
Opaque software is software that hides what it does and why. It is difficult to
understand, difficult to debug, and difficult to modify. Transparency is closely
related to simplicity, but it is not the same thing. A simple program may still be
opaque if its internals are hidden. A transparent program is one whose workings are
visible and clear.

Rule of Robustness: Robustness is the child of transparency and simplicity.

Software is robust when it behaves well even in unexpected situations. Robustness
requires that the software be designed to handle errors gracefully. It requires that
the software be designed to degrade gracefully when resources are limited. It
requires that the software recover from failures when possible. Robustness is not
achieved by adding lots of error-handling code. It is achieved by designing software
that is inherently simple and transparent, so that the common case is always handled
correctly.

Rule of Representation: Fold knowledge into data so program logic can be stupid and
robust.

Data is more tractable than program logic. It follows that where you see a choice
between complexity in data structures and complexity in code, choose to make the data
structures more elaborate. Complex data structures are easy to inspect and reason
about. Complex code is not. This principle is sometimes called the "smart data,
dumb code" principle.

Rule of Least Surprise: In interface design, always do the least surprising thing.

The most important rule of interface design is that you should do the least surprising
thing. Users come to new software with expectations formed by their experience with
other software. If your software violates those expectations, the users will be
confused and frustrated. They will make mistakes. They will not be able to use your
software effectively. Doing the least surprising thing means conforming to existing
conventions when they exist, and choosing the most natural and intuitive behavior
when they do not.

Rule of Silence: When a program has nothing surprising to say, it should say nothing.

Well-behaved Unix programs do their jobs quietly. They do not chatter unnecessarily.
They do not print progress messages unless the user has asked for them. They do not
print warnings unless there is a real problem. They do not print informational messages
unless the user needs that information. This principle of silence is closely related
to the principle of least surprise. Users do not want to be surprised by unexpected
output, even if that output is informative.

Rule of Repair: Repair what you can — but when you must fail, fail noisily and as
soon as possible.

Software should try to recover from errors when it can, but when it cannot, it should
fail quickly and clearly. A program that silently corrupts data is far worse than a
program that loudly announces failure and stops. Silent failures are the hardest to
debug because there is no error message to guide the developer to the source of the
problem. Loud failures are easy to debug because the error message points directly
to the problem.

Rule of Economy: Programmer time is expensive; conserve it in preference to machine
time.

In the early days of Unix, computer time was expensive and programmer time was cheap.
Today the situation is reversed. Machine cycles are essentially free. Programmer time
is expensive. This means that it is better to write software that is easy to
understand and maintain, even if that software is slightly less efficient in its use
of machine resources. The cost of programmer time to understand and fix complex
software far exceeds the cost of machine time to run slightly inefficient simple
software.

Rule of Generation: Avoid hand-hacking; write programs to write programs when you
can.

One of the most powerful ideas in Unix programming is the use of programs to generate
other programs. Compilers, interpreters, code generators, preprocessors — all of
these are programs that write programs. Using these tools can dramatically reduce
the amount of code that humans must write and maintain. Generated code is less prone
to errors than hand-written code. It is easier to change the generator than to
change all the instances of generated code scattered through a large codebase.

Rule of Optimization: Prototype before polishing. Get it working before you optimize
it.

The most common mistake in performance optimization is to optimize before profiling.
Developers who optimize before profiling often optimize the wrong things. They spend
time making minor optimizations to code that is not on the critical path. Meanwhile
the actual bottlenecks remain. The correct approach is to write a working prototype
first, then profile it to find the real bottlenecks, then optimize only those
bottlenecks.

Rule of Diversity: Distrust all claims for one true way.

Unix culture is suspicious of monocultures. The Unix philosophy encourages
diversity of approaches. Different problems call for different solutions. Different
users have different needs. A healthy software ecosystem contains many tools, each
good at a particular job, rather than one monolithic tool that tries to do everything.
This diversity makes the system more resilient and more adaptable.

Rule of Extensibility: Design for the future, because it will be here sooner than you
think.

Good software design anticipates the need for change. It provides hooks for extension.
It separates the stable core from the variable periphery. It makes it easy to add
new features without breaking existing functionality. Extensible software has a
longer useful life than software that is designed for a fixed set of requirements.
